steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure on each side of the blank
steps.content:
On the left: “Only five of these dances are included in international ballroom dance competitions” is a complete independent clause (subject: “five,” verb: “are included”).

On the right: After the list comes a relative clause—“the last of which is grouped with the other Latin dances despite not having Latin roots.” This clause carries the main idea, with “the last of which” as the subject and “is grouped” as the verb. Even though the sentence lists the five dances first, the second thought conveys a complete idea: [the five are] rumba, samba, cha-cha-cha, paso doble, and jive, the last of which [is grouped with the other Latin dances].

steps.title: Apply the rule for conjunctive adverbs
steps.content:
When "however" connects two related independent clauses or thoughts, the standard rule is: semicolon before the conjunctive adverb, comma after. Choice D places the semicolon before "however" and a comma after it—exactly this pattern: competitions; however, [second thought]. This punctuation correctly signals that the clause following "however" is a related independent thought, even though the subject is implied.

Choices A, B, and C do not follow this standard pattern: A adds a colon after "however" (nonstandard), B treats "however" as merely parenthetical (loses the contrasting force), and C mixes punctuation marks incorrectly.

That matches choice D — D is correct.
